%% The abstract is a short summary of the work to be presented in the
%% article.
\begin{abstract}
Robotic arm manipulation has advanced rapidly with the emergence of deep learning, foundation models, and large language models, giving rise to a diverse landscape of learning paradigms and system designs. 
This survey takes embodiment constraints as the starting point, tracing how they shape the development of embodied intelligence across data and knowledge support, learning paradigms, and evaluation.
Specifically, these constraints first define the conditions of data and knowledge support, then structure learning paradigms, and ultimately condition evaluation throughout the development of embodied arm manipulation.
By tracing coupled pressures across the full stack, from low-level operating systems to fine-grained evaluation metrics, this survey aims to provide a more structured understanding of robotic arm manipulation within a large, complex, and highly heterogeneous research landscape, and to clarify the central challenges for building scalable, deployable, and trustworthy robotic arm manipulation systems.
\end{abstract}

\section{Introduction}\label{sec:introduction}

Embodied intelligence starts from the premise that cognition is shaped by the dynamic coupling of brain, body, and environment~\cite{clarkBeingTherePutting1998}. 
In robotics, this premise requires intelligent behavior to be grounded in the continuous coupling of perception, action, and physical interaction in the real world~\cite{brooksIntelligenceWithoutRepresentation1991}.
Robotic arm manipulation provides a concrete and demanding setting for embodied intelligence, as it concentrates the constraints of perception, action, contact dynamics, and high-DoF control within a single physical system~\cite{khatibInertialPropertiesRobot1987}.
Within this setting, successive advances in deep learning and, more recently, foundation models have progressively reshaped the methodological landscape of robotic arm manipulation. 
However, this transformation was not driven by a single model lineage. 
Rather, it emerged from the co-evolution of robot embodiment, scalable data pipelines, learning paradigms, and evaluation benchmarks. 
As summarized in Figure~\ref{fig:cross_layer_timeline}, robotic arm manipulation evolved from early middleware and simulation platforms~\cite{macenski2022robot,todorov2012mujoco}, 
to scalable demonstration and multi-robot datasets~\cite{mandlekar2018roboturk,dasari2020robonet,padalkar2023open}, 
to data-driven policy learning~\cite{brohan2023rt1,chi2023diffusion}, 
and more recently to vision-language-action and generalist manipulation models~\cite{brohan2023rt2,kim2024openvla,black2024pi0}. 
These transitions across embodied foundations, data, learning paradigms, and system evaluation motivate us to provide a unified understanding of robotic arm manipulation through each stage of the research stack.
Several recent surveys cover adjacent parts of this landscape, including unified abstractions for robotic arm manipulation~\cite{baiUnifiedUnderstandingRobot2025}, review of vision-language-action (VLA) models for real-world robotics~\cite{kawaharazukaVisionLanguageActionModelsRobotics2025}, object-centric embodied learning~\cite{survey2025objectcentric}, and broader embodied AI systems built on LLMs and world models~\cite{liuEmbodiedIntelligenceSynergy2025}. 
While these surveys provide valuable coverage of models, tasks, and embodied AI systems, they usually do not place the specific embodiment of robotic arms at the center of comparison.
As a result, methods developed in separate subfields are often framed around different problem definitions, making them difficult to compare within a unified structure.
Local methodological variants may then be treated as independent categories, producing taxonomies that are more complex and less interpretable than necessary.
Therefore, this survey examines robotic arm manipulation toward embodied intelligence through the lens of embodiment constraints, emphasizing how physical embodiment, perception, action, and control jointly shape data, learning, and evaluation.

\textbf{Contribution:}
From this perspective, the survey makes three closely connected contributions.
First, it explains robotic arm manipulation from the perspective of embodiment, showing how physical embodiment, perception, action, and control jointly shape data collection, policy learning, deployment, and evaluation.
Second, it develops a layered taxonomy spanning data support, learning paradigms, and system evaluation, enabling independently framed solutions to be categorized according to the underlying constraints they address.
Third, it uses this organization to reveal how open challenges accumulate across the full manipulation pipeline, from embodiment foundations and data ecosystems to learning architectures, evaluation, and trustworthy deployment.

\textbf{Structure of the Paper:}
Section~\ref{sec:embodiment_hardware} reviews physical embodiment and software infrastructure. Section~\ref{sec:embodiment_dataset} examines the data ecosystem from datasets through acquisition, synthesis, refinement, and pipelines. Section~\ref{sec:embodiment_method} analyzes cognitive planning, policy learning, integration models, and deployment. Section~\ref{sec:evaluation} synthesizes evaluation methodologies, and Section~\ref{sec:challenges} concludes with open challenges and future directions.

\section{Embodiment Foundations for Robotic Arm Manipulation}\label{sec:embodiment_hardware}

For robotic arm platforms, the foundations of manipulation lie in two aspects of embodiment. 
Physical embodiment defines how interaction is physically structured for robotic arm manipulation through contact, sensing, action, and platform constraints. 
Software framework makes these physically grounded capabilities operational by supporting data capture, communication, low-level control, and the formalization of robotic experience for downstream learning.
The rest of this section is organized accordingly. Section~\ref{sec:embodiment_physical} and Section~\ref{sec:embodiment_software} examine these two aspects in turn.

\subsection{Physical Embodiment}\label{sec:embodiment_physical}

Physical embodiment shapes robotic arm manipulation before learning starts. 
It determines how contact can be made, sensed, and transformed into controllable behavior.
We begin with end-effectors and sensors, then turn to robotic arms as the platforms that organize multi-DoF motion into executable manipulation, and finally discuss how arms are integrated into larger mobile and whole-body systems.
Figure~\ref{fig:embodiment_taxonomy} summarizes this taxonomy. 
From this perspective, physical embodiment can be seen as the source of the constraints that define manipulation objectives, data requirements, and methodological choices.

\subsubsection{End-effectors and Sensors}\label{subsec:end_effectors}

\begin{figure}[!t]
    \centering
    \includegraphics[width=\linewidth,trim=0cm 5.3cm 0cm 5.3cm,clip]{assets/embodiment.pdf}
    \caption{Overview of physical embodiments for robotic arm manipulation. The figure covers end-effectors, sensors, stationary platforms, and mobile platforms.}
    \Description{A hierarchical diagram showing the overview of physical embodiments for robotic arm manipulation. End-effectors are classified by contact modality (jaw gripper, dexterous hand, soft hand) and sensing (vision, tactile); platforms are divided into stationary (single-arm, bimanual) and mobile (wheeled, quadrupedal, humanoid).}\label{fig:embodiment_taxonomy}
\end{figure}


End-effectors shape the contact space of manipulation by determining how contact is formed, maintained, and transformed during interaction.
Different end-effector designs therefore differ not only in capability, but in the contact regime through which manipulation is organized.
Jaw grippers, such as the OnRobot RG2/RG6 series, favor stable and mechanically constrained contact, making grasping, alignment, and release easier to control and predict.
Dexterous hands, such as the SCHUNK SVH and Tesollo DG-5F-M, allow contacts to change more freely during manipulation and support richer in-hand interaction, but they also make behavior harder to reduce to a small set of repeatable contact patterns.
Soft hands, such as SpiRobs~\cite{lee2023spirobs}, address uncertainty differently by using compliance to absorb part of the interaction variability in the hardware itself.
Sensors shape the observation space of manipulation by providing access to task-relevant information during execution.
The central task of sensors is to ensure the information required at different stages of interaction is observable.
Before contact, the dominant uncertainties concern object geometry, pose, and scene layout, so visual sensing is often sufficient for coarse localization, alignment, and approach. 
During contact, however, task-relevant information becomes increasingly local and interaction-dependent, including slip, deformation, insertion error, and force misalignment. 
These states are often critical to task success, yet they are only weakly reflected in external visual observations. 
For this reason, tactile, force, and proprioceptive sensing become critical once task-relevant information is expressed primarily through contact itself. 
Sensors such as GelSight~\cite{yuan2017gelsight}, DIGIT~\cite{mike2020digit}, and IMU make different portions of this observation space accessible by exposing local contact geometry, contact transients, and motion signals that are difficult to recover from external vision alone.
End-effectors and sensors shape how a robotic arm interacts with its environment. 
Through contact space and observation space, they bound the otherwise open-ended complexity of manipulation into structured constraints, making the discussion that follows more tractable.

\subsubsection{Fixed-base Robotic Arms}\label{subsec:manipulators}

In common fixed-base arm manipulation settings, controllable multi-DoF motion is used to position end-effectors and sensors during task execution.
They organize motor commands into structured sequences of actions. 
Through active motion and contact, these action sequences induce changes in task state and observation during execution.
Hence, we will focus on how robotic arms transform motor commands into reliable action sequences.
In single-arm settings, such as KUKA LBR iiwa~\cite{mokaram2017rosiiwa}, the action structure is relatively localized. 
It is typically organized around a compact set of objectives through motor commands, including establishing a stable grasp, aligning the object to a desired pose, and completing a local contact transition.
Since their execution structure is relatively localized and compact, motion sequences and state transitions in single-arm settings are relatively easier to collect, model, and evaluate. 
Consequently, single-arm systems remain the dominant setting for teleoperation, policy benchmarking, and manipulation with high precision.
In bimanual settings, such as PR2~\cite{wyrobek2008personal}, manipulation requires coordination across multiple arms.
Once multiple manipulators act on the same object, the task becomes coordinated transformation under shared kinematic and force constraints.
This coordination enables the system to perform tasks that require coordinated force regulation or the handling of large, heavy, or deformable objects.
As a result, bimanual systems are increasingly used in assembly, tool use, and human-robot collaboration.
These manipulation settings arise from practical task demands, and in turn become part of what shapes and constrains the embodied intelligence.
Action sequences, together with the state transitions they induce, will form an important part of data support discussed in Section~\ref{sec:embodiment_dataset}.

\subsubsection{Mobile Platforms}\label{subsec:mobile_platforms}

Mounting robotic arms on mobile platforms expands the scope of manipulation.
In wheeled mobile manipulators such as Mobile ALOHA~\cite{fu2024mobile}, Stretch~\cite{chase2025dynamics}, arm action needs to coordinate with base motion and navigation constraints. 
In legged platforms and humanoids, manipulation needs to be executed together with balance, posture regulation, and system-level support conditions. 
In conclusion, robotic arms, as a central component of robotic arm manipulation, are increasingly being integrated into larger support systems, thereby exhibiting greater capability and broader potential.

\subsection{Software Framework}\label{sec:embodiment_software}

The software framework of robotic arm manipulation provides the infrastructure that makes physically grounded capabilities operational in practice.
During runtime, it supports signal capture and transport, coordinates communication across distributed components, and sustains low-level control. 
Beyond execution, it also defines how robotic experience is recorded and structured for downstream use.

\subsubsection{Modern Robotics Middleware and Operating Systems}\label{subsec:control_middleware}

The software stack of robotic arm manipulation systems exists to make embodiment data accessible: ensuring signals are captured with sufficient temporal fidelity, transported without bottleneck, and organized at scale across heterogeneous hardware and compute.
We organize this stack into three tiers, as summarized in Table~\ref{tab:framework_comparison}. 
The Capture Layer (L0) ensures the temporal determinism required for reliable signal acquisition from sensors and actuators. 
Industrial stacks rely on real-time operating system (RTOS) kernels such as VxWorks~\cite{xu2014mpi} and QNX~\cite{dan1992architectural} for bounded interrupt latency and fault isolation, while Zephyr extends the same guarantees to edge devices.
The Transfer Layer (L1) moves captured data between processes, devices, and model components. 
ROS~2 provides the baseline connectivity through decentralized Data Distribution Service (DDS)~\cite{macenski2022robot}, while the growing cost of moving multimodal embodiment data has driven zero-copy designs such as Dora-rs~\cite{zhang2026dora}.
The Orchestration Layer (L2) organizes data flows across robots, edge devices, and cloud services at the system level. 
Zenoh-Flow and FogROS2 are designed with deployment, routing, and compute offloading as core system concerns~\cite{baldoni2023zenohflow, ichnowski2022fogros2}, while RoboOS couples multimodal planning to reusable skill libraries across heterogeneous embodiments~\cite{wang2025roboos}.

\begin{table}[!t]
\centering
\caption{\textbf{Three-tier software architecture of robotic arm manipulation for embodied intelligence.}
The software stack is organized into three layers. L0 is the Capture Layer for hard real-time motor control with microsecond-level determinism. L1 is the Transfer Layer for high-throughput, low-latency inter-process and inter-device communication. L2 is the Orchestration Layer for distributed orchestration across robots, edge devices, cloud resources, and fleets. The Profile column reports interrupt or IPC latency for L0 and L1 systems, and deployment or orchestration characteristics for L2 systems.}\label{tab:framework_comparison}

\subsubsection{Low-level Motion Planning and Control}\label{subsec:lowlevel_control}

While middleware provides the infrastructure for data capture and transport, low-level motion planning and control determine how physical embodiment is actuated during execution. 
For end-effector interaction, impedance control enables compliant interaction by regulating the relationship between positional deviation and contact force, and it is the standard approach for assembly, tool use, and other forms of contact-rich manipulation. 
For fixed-base arms, this layer resolves operational objectives into joint motions and torques through kinematic mapping, rigid-body dynamic compensation, and enforcement of joint and collision constraints.
For mobile platforms and humanoids, where manipulation is coupled with locomotion, balance, and whole-body motion, MPC and WBC extend this control layer in complementary ways. 
MPC optimizes future motion, contact, and stability objectives over a receding horizon, while WBC maps task, posture, contact, and feasibility objectives into coordinated whole-body commands under kinematic and dynamic constraints~\cite{katayama2023model}.
Beyond execution, software frameworks also define how robotic experience is recorded and structured for downstream use. 
Common formats range from runtime logs and telemetry records to trajectory dataset schemas such as RLDS and HDF5-based demonstrations, which organize observations, actions, rewards, and metadata for imitation learning and offline reinforcement learning.

\section{Data Ecosystem for Robotic Arm Manipulation}\label{sec:embodiment_dataset}

\begin{figure}[!t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\linewidth,trim=0cm 0.8cm 1.5cm 0.5cm,clip]{assets/datasupport.pdf}
  \caption{Overview of the embodied data ecosystem, organized into embodied datasets, data acquisition, data refinement, and data pipelines. The graph shows how data are collected, structured, refined, and incorporated into downstream learning and deployment pipelines.}
  \Description{A hierarchical diagram showing the organization of the survey on embodied AI for robotic arms. The top node branches into four main pillars: system architecture, data ecosystem, architectural paradigms, and evaluation metrics. These pillars further expand into subtopics including embodied systems, dataset categories, data refinement, cognitive planning, policy learning, integration models, deployment lifecycle, and evaluation criteria. Arrows indicate conceptual dependencies across the survey structure.}\label{fig:data_support}
\end{figure}

Having clarified how embodiment constrains robotic arm manipulation through contact, observation, and execution, we now turn to the data ecosystem through which these constraints are captured, formalized, and made learnable. 
In this sense, data connect embodied foundations to the emergence of embodied intelligence.
We denote robotic manipulation data by $\mathcal{D}$, which records the structured traces of embodied interaction as the robot engages with the world.
Concretely, $\mathcal{D}$ comprises four components: \textbf{motion sequences} generated by the robotic arm, \textbf{sensory observations} provided by onboard or external sensors, \textbf{contact events} arising through the end-effector, and the resulting \textbf{state transitions} induced during execution.
From the perspective of embodied intelligence, progress in the data ecosystem can be understood in terms of how $\mathcal{D}$ is organized, collected, refined, and reused across tasks, scenes, and embodiments.
Organized around $\mathcal{D}$, this chapter first reviews embodied datasets in Section~\ref{sec:eai_datasets}, and then examines the broader processes through which robotic experience is produced and operationalized, including data acquisition in Section~\ref{sec:data_acquisition}, data refinement in Section~\ref{sec:data_refinement}, and large-scale data pipelines in Section~\ref{sec:data_factories_robotic_arms}. 
Together, these components provide a structured account of how data support is formed under embodied constraints for robotic arm manipulation, as summarized in Figure~\ref{fig:data_support}.

\subsection{Embodied Datasets}\label{sec:eai_datasets}

Through a systematic review of the literature, we observe that robotic arm manipulation datasets have evolved toward progressively broader forms of support for learning: from preserving local interaction structure, to capturing how robotic manipulation unfolds over temporally extended execution, and ultimately to supporting transfer across heterogeneous embodiments.
We abstract this progression into three increasingly broad tiers of embodied datasets: skill-level datasets ($\mathcal{D}_1$), which preserve local interaction structure; task-level datasets ($\mathcal{D}_2$), which preserve these interactions over temporally extended execution; and foundation-level datasets ($\mathcal{D}_3$), which preserve transfer across heterogeneous embodiments at scale.
In the remainder of this section, we review robotic arm manipulation datasets according to these three levels,Section~\ref{sec:skill_datasets_narrative} for skill-level datasets, Section~\ref{sec:task_datasets_narrative} for task-level datasets, and Section~\ref{sec:foundation_datasets} for foundation-level datasets.
As the scope of $\mathcal{D}$ expands across these tiers, the implied knowledge support $\mathcal{K}$ expands accordingly.
We will discuss the role of $\mathcal{K}$, and how it supports different learning paradigms, in greater detail in Chapter~\ref{sec:embodiment_method}.

\textbf{DexGraspNet 2.0}~\cite{zhang2024dexgraspnet20learninggenerative} & 2024 & Dexterous Grasp & Multi-Object & 5,355 & Sim & 427M grasps & Point Cloud & $\times$ \\
\textbf{Dex1B}~\cite{ye2025dex1b} & 2025 & Dexterous Grasp & Single Object & 1M & Sim & 1B grasps & Point Cloud & $\times$ \\
\midrule
\multicolumn{9}{c}{\textbf{Language-Grounded Grasping}} \\
\midrule
\textbf{Grasp-Anything}~\cite{vuong2023graspanything} & 2023 & Rect.\ Grasp + Push & Multi-Object & 3M & Syn & 1M imgs (600M grasps) & RGB & $\checkmark$ \\
\textbf{ReasoningGrasp}~\cite{jin2024reasoninggrasp} & 2024 & 6-DoF Grasp & Multi-Object & 64 & Real & 99M grasps & RGB-D & $\checkmark$ \\
\midrule
\multicolumn{9}{c}{\textbf{Diverse Motor Primitives}} \\
\midrule
\textbf{Omnipush}~\cite{bauza2021omnipush} & 2019 & Pushing (Planar) & Single Object & 250 & Real & 62.5k pushes & RGB-D & $\times$ \\
\textbf{PartNet-Mobility}~\cite{xiang2020sapien} & 2020 & Articulated (Open/Close) & Single Object & 2,346 models & Sim & 2,346 articulated assets & 3D / PC & $\times$ \\
\textbf{FurnitureBench}~\cite{heo2023furniturebench} & 2023 & Assembly (Precise) & Tabletop & Furniture & Sim+Real & 5.1k demos (220 hrs) & RGB & $\times$ \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
}
\end{table}

\subsubsection{Skill-level Datasets}\label{sec:skill_datasets_narrative}

The development of skill-level datasets reflects a steady expansion of \(\mathcal{D}_1\), from planar grasp annotations, to dense 6-DoF grasp synthesis, to dexterous hand configurations and other interactions such as pushing, articulation, and assembly. 
Table~\ref{tab:skill_datasets} lists representative datasets along this tier.
Early grasping benchmarks such as Cornell~\cite{lenz2014deep} labeled graspable regions as 2D oriented rectangles, covering vertical parallel-jaw approaches.
Dex-Net 2.0~\cite{mahler2017dex} generated 6.7 million synthetic grasp examples labeled with analytic grasp metrics, enabling large-scale grasp learning without collecting training data through physical trials.
GraspNet-1B~\cite{fang2020graspnet} annotated 1.2 billion 6-DoF grasps across cluttered real-world scenes, and GraspClutter6D~\cite{back2025graspclutter6d} extended this to denser clutter configurations.
For multi-fingered hands ($>$20 DoF), DexGraspNet 2.0~\cite{zhang2024dexgraspnet20learninggenerative} synthesized 427M grasps via diffusion-based generation (90.7\% real-world success), and Dex1B~\cite{ye2025dex1b} reached one billion grasps.
Tactile modalities complement vision: T-DEX~\cite{guzey2023tdex} showed that 2.5 hours of self-supervised tactile play data improves dexterous task success by 1.7$\times$ over baselines with only vision, capturing slip and reorientation signals invisible to cameras.
Beyond grasping, Omnipush~\cite{bauza2021omnipush} records planar pushing dynamics across 250 objects, PartNet-Mobility~\cite{xiang2020sapien} provides part interaction data for articulated objects, and FurnitureBench~\cite{heo2023furniturebench} supplies 5,000 teleoperated assembly trajectories with a paired simulator.

\subsubsection{Task-level Datasets}\label{sec:task_datasets_narrative}

Task-level datasets evolved \(\mathcal{D}_2\) by extending isolated motor primitives into trajectories aligned with language instructions, gradually increasing semantic coverage, environmental diversity, and calibration quality across collection sites. 
Table~\ref{tab:task_datasets} lists representative datasets along this tier.
MT-Opt~\cite{kalashnikov2021mt} collected 800k trajectories through autonomous exploration but used task IDs to index actions, without language supervision. 
BC-Z~\cite{jang2022bc} introduced language-conditioned supervision by pairing 26k trajectories with 100 semantic verbs, and RT-1 Dataset~\cite{brohan2023rt1} extended this to 130k trajectories spanning 700+ instructions. 
However, despite their growing scale, these early datasets were collected in a limited range of settings within one or a few research institutions, which restricted environmental diversity.
BridgeData V2~\cite{walke2024bridgedatav2} widened scene coverage to 24 environments via collection in several locations, 
DROID~\cite{khazatsky2024droid} coordinated 18 institutions across 564 scenes with calibrated extrinsics, 
and Dobb-E~\cite{shafiullah2023bringing} accessed 22 domestic environments using only an iPhone mounted on a Stretch robot. 
FMB~\cite{fu2025fmb} added force-torque calibration across 22k contact-rich assembly trajectories.

\subsubsection{Foundation-level Datasets}\label{sec:foundation_datasets}

Foundation-level datasets expand \(\mathcal{D}_3\) by organizing robot experience across platforms into a unified layer of support for cross-embodiment learning, with increasing emphasis on alignment, standardization, and multimodal consistency.
Table~\ref{tab:foundation_datasets} summarizes representative datasets in this tier.
RoboNet~\cite{dasari2020robonet} established an early multi-robot dataset with 15 million video frames collected across seven robot platforms.
OXE~\cite{padalkar2023open} scaled this direction by unifying over one million trajectories from 22 robot platforms through the RLDS protocol.
As this tier scales, standardization and multimodal alignment become equally important.
ARIO~\cite{wang2024ario} provides a unified large-scale dataset with approximately three million episodes and consistent timestamp alignment across heterogeneous sources, while RH-20T~\cite{fang2023rh} contributes over 110k contact-rich manipulation sequences with synchronized visual, force, audio, and action signals.
RoboMIND~\cite{wu2025robomind} extends this tier toward standardized multi-robot task datasets, pairing 107k trajectories across four robot types with failure annotations, and RoboMIND 2.0~\cite{hou2026robomind20} scales this to over 310k trajectories across six robot platforms and 739 tasks.
AgiBot World Beta~\cite{agibot2025} pushes this tier toward humanoid and dual-arm manipulation, providing over one million trajectories from 100 robots across 100+ real-world scenarios.
As robot data demand outpaces collection speed, some pipelines supplement trajectory datasets with egocentric human video such as Ego-Exo4D~\cite{grauman2024egoexo4d}.

\subsection{Data Acquisition}\label{sec:data_acquisition}

The previous section distinguished dataset tiers by the support they provide. 
However, acquiring such support is not straightforward. 
Unlike generic datasets, embodied manipulation data need to record action sequences under the constraints of observation space and contact space. 
Collecting data that is both physically grounded and rich enough to support learning at scale is difficult. 
This section therefore examines three main routes by which embodied interaction is converted into \(\mathcal{D}\) through real-world collection in Section~\ref{sec:real_acquisition}, simulation ecosystems in Section~\ref{sec:simulation_infrastructure}, and automatic data generation in Section~\ref{sec:auto_data_gen}. 

\subsubsection{Real-world Data Gathering}\label{sec:real_acquisition}

Since real-world collection is conducted under concrete hardware and scene constraints, its efficiency and coverage are constrained by the robotic arm platform, end-effectors, and execution environments. 
Recent research can be read as a gradual effort to weaken this coupling while retaining useful observation, contact, and execution structure. 
Direct teleoperation on the deployment robot provides a straightforward way to acquire such data, and it forms the basis of many early approaches.
RoboTurk~\cite{mandlekar2018roboturk} built a teleoperation interface that enabled demonstrations to be collected from geographically distributed users over the internet. 
ALOHA~\cite{zhao2023learning, fu2024mobile} introduced a low-cost teleoperation setup for bimanual and later mobile whole-body manipulation, making long-horizon and contact-rich data collection more accessible. 
Teleoperation preserves the coupling between observation space, contact space, and arm-centric execution most faithfully, but it is also tightly constrained by hardware access and human labor. 
For this reason, later work moves toward more portable and reusable data-collection interfaces, so that demonstrations can be transferred more easily across robot embodiments and deployment setups.
UMI~\cite{chi2024umi} used handheld grippers and consumer cameras to capture demonstrations in everyday environments and then retargeted them offline to the target robot platform, 
while DexCap~\cite{wang2024dexcap} pushed this approach toward dexterous manipulation by recording high-dimensional human hand motion for transfer to multi-fingered robotic manipulators.
Even so, these interfaces are limited to narrower robotic arms and still rely on teleoperation. 
This motivates a further step toward egocentric and video-based supervision by retargeting human demonstrations back into robotic arm execution.
EgoMimic~\cite{kareer2025egomimic} used egocentric human video to enable large-scale data capture without requiring robot hardware during collection, 
and AV-ALOHA~\cite{chuang2025avaloha} showed that such data become more useful when the demonstrator's viewpoint is perceptually aligned with the robot's onboard observation space. 

\subsubsection{Simulation Data Ecosystems}\label{sec:simulation_infrastructure}

\centering

\resizebox{\textwidth}{!}{%

\toprule
\textbf{Simulator} & \textbf{Features} & \textbf{Capabilities \& Modalities} & \textbf{Key Benchmarks \& Datasets} \\
\midrule

\midrule
\textbf{MuJoCo} & Stable analytic contact solver; MJX hardware acceleration; Differentiable dynamics. & \textbf{Physical Aligned Data}: Trajectories for fine-grained motor control.

\midrule

\multicolumn{4}{c}{\textbf{\textbf{II\@. Specialized Task Platforms}}} \\
\midrule
\textbf{PyBullet / CoppeliaSim} & Lightweight legacy physics; Stable IK solvers; Hardware and ROS abstraction. & \textbf{Algorithmic Baseline Data}: Logic planning and action primitives. 

\textbf{Modalities: RGB-D, Joint states, Contact forces, Proximity sensors, Force/Torque.} & \textbf{CALVIN}~\cite{mees2022calvin}, \textbf{RLBench}~\cite{james2020rlbench} \\

\textbf{Habitat / AI2-THOR} & Procedural indoor scene generation; Scalable visual navigation environments. & \textbf{Embodied Navigation Data}: Semantic search and indoor reasoning. 

\textbf{Modalities: RGB-D, Segmentations, Object states, Agent pose, Audio.} & OpenEQA~\cite{majumdar2024openeqa}, ALFRED~\cite{shridhar2020alfred} \\

\bottomrule
\end{tabular}}
\end{table}

Compared with real-world collection, simulation weakens the dependence of data acquisition on specific robotic arm platforms, end-effector and sensor setups, and scene conditions. 
This makes it easier to define different formulations of \(\mathcal{D}\) for robotic arm manipulation under more explicit assumptions.
In this section, MuJoCo, SAPIEN, and NVIDIA Isaac are treated as three representative directions because they align naturally with different tiers of \(\mathcal{D}\)  for robotic arm manipulation, while other platforms serve for specialized applications. Table~\ref{tab:sim_classification} summarizes these design tendencies.
MuJoCo~\cite{todorov2012mujoco} is well aligned with \(\mathcal{D}_1\) data, where stable local interaction and arm-centric rollouts matter most for robotic arm execution.
Meta-World~\cite{yu2021meta} introduced a widely used simulated benchmark for multi-task and meta-reinforcement learning across 50 robotic manipulation tasks.
Later benchmarks such as LIBERO~\cite{liu2023libero} and RoboCasa~\cite{nasiriany2024robocasa} added lifelong-learning curricula and richer household scenes. 
MJX and MuJoCo Playground support large-batch rollouts, differentiable simulation, and sim-to-real analysis within the same stack~\cite{deepmind2025playground}.
SAPIEN~\cite{xiang2020sapien} is particularly suitable for \(\mathcal{D}_2\) support, as articulated object states and part-level annotations support structured, temporally extended interaction modeling for robot manipulation.
PartNet-Mobility provides articulated assets with annotated joints, ManiSkill builds benchmark suites on this basis~\cite{tao2025maniskill3}, 
and SIMPLER uses the same infrastructure for sim-to-real evaluation~\cite{li2025simpler}.
NVIDIA Isaac is the platform best aligned with \(\mathcal{D}_3\), particularly in settings that demand multimodality, scale, and broad platform coverage~\cite{mittal2023orbit}. 
Representative embodied benchmarks built on NVIDIA Isaac Sim include BEHAVIOR-1K, which targets large-scale household activities, 
and ARNOLD, which focuses on language-grounded manipulation with continuous object states~\cite{li2024behavior1k, gong2023arnold}.
Beyond these three ecosystems, several other platforms are still used for specialized applications of robotic arm manipulation.
PyBullet and CoppeliaSim support manipulation baselines such as RLBench and CALVIN~\cite{james2020rlbench, mees2022calvin}.
Habitat and AI2-THOR help compensate for the limited semantic support available in mainstream manipulation simulators, supporting benchmarks such as ALFRED and OpenEQA~\cite{shridhar2020alfred, majumdar2024openeqa}.
SoftGym supports deformable object manipulation benchmarks that rigid-body simulators still capture only weakly~\cite{lin2021softgym}.
By reducing dependence on physical robot hardware, simulation improves experimental efficiency and allows more effort to be directed toward algorithmic questions above the arm hardware layer. 
However, simulation cannot fully capture real-world physics and hardware-specific behavior, and the target arm itself is not involved in the data collection loop.
Consequently, the resulting \(\mathcal{D}\) for robotic arm learning is often not fully faithful to robotic manipulation, leading to a substantial sim-to-real gap.

\subsubsection{Automatic Data Generation}\label{sec:auto_data_gen}

Building on the environments discussed above,
Automatic data generation synthesizes different parts of \(\mathcal{D}\), 
we discuss automatic data generation in three forms: scene generation synthesizes sensory observations, 
demonstration synthesis generates motion sequences together with contact events, and generative world models synthesize state transitions along with their future rollouts.
Scene generation synthesizes sensory observations by widening the scene and semantic conditions under which they are produced within the observation space.
ProcTHOR~\cite{deitke2022procthor} generated large-scale procedural indoor environments, 
Holodeck~\cite{yang2024holodeck} introduced semantic scene generation for embodied environments,
and RoboGen~\cite{wang2024robogen} connected asset retrieval, scene construction, and policy training within one automated pipeline.
Demonstration synthesis generates motion sequences together with contact events by generating additional robotic arm trajectories from limited source demonstrations or task specifications.
MimicGen~\cite{mandlekar2023mimicgen} showed that one demonstration can be expanded into many valid trajectories through spatial transformation, 
Tether~\cite{liang2026tether} further broadens demonstration synthesis into autonomous trajectory generation,
and GenSim~\cite{wang2024gensim} moved this idea to task generation by using LLMs to produce simulation code and reward structure.
Generative world models synthesize state transitions along with their future rollouts by learning predictive models that can synthesize additional transitions from existing data.
Cosmos~\cite{nvidia2025cosmos} extends this paradigm to web-scale video data, training video foundation models to acquire physical priors from large-scale visual experience.
IRASim~\cite{zhu2025irasim} brings this into the real-robot setting through video world modeling of fine-grained robotic manipulation.
DreamGen uses learned world models as both data generators and training environments~\cite{jang2025dreamge}. 
Automatic data generation, however, synthesizes \(\mathcal{D}\) by recombining or rolling forward an existing environment. 
The errors and limitations of that environment are also amplified.

\subsection{Data Refinement}\label{sec:data_refinement}

In real-world settings, available \(\mathcal{D}\) is often still limited in scale and uneven in quality, which motivates further data refinement and augmentation. 
We organize these methods around four mechanisms: visual and viewpoint augmentation in Section~\ref{sec:trajectory_redrawing} broadens sensory observations, 
physically grounded augmentation in Section~\ref{sec:dynamics_constraints} preserves the executability of motion sequences together with contact events under kinematic and contact constraints, 
correction and recovery augmentation in Section~\ref{sec:correction_recovery} supplements state transitions with the failure-recovery data, 
and data curation and verification in Section~\ref{sec:data_curation} determine whether existing \(\mathcal{D}\) is still usable for learning and how low-quality data can be filtered, reweighted, or curated. 

\subsubsection{Visual and Viewpoint Augmentation}\label{sec:trajectory_redrawing}

Visual and viewpoint augmentation expands existing demonstrations along two main axes: semantic appearance variation and camera transformation, both without requiring new robot rollouts. 
Early work such as GenAug and ROSIE used generative image models to vary textures, objects, and scene composition in order to widen the visual distribution seen during imitation learning~\cite{chen2023genaug, yu2023scalingrobotlearningsemantically}. 
Later methods moved beyond appearance randomization to augmentations that explicitly model changes in camera viewpoint: RoVi-Aug augments both robot appearance and camera pose while preserving action labels and contact consistency under novel views~\cite{chen2025roviaug}. 
A complementary line extends supervision beyond robot demonstrations: VISTA~\cite{wang2024vista} enables multi-view synthesis from a single view for policy learning, H2R~\cite{li2025h2r} converts egocentric human video into robot-centric pretraining data by replacing human hands with rendered end-effectors, and Pan et al.~\cite{pan2025one} combine novel view synthesis with trajectory warping to derive diverse training trajectories from a single demonstration.

\subsubsection{Physically Grounded Augmentation}\label{sec:dynamics_constraints}

Augmenting physical trajectories (e.g., shifting a grasp 5\,cm laterally) requires that the result still obey kinematic limits, collision constraints, and contact dynamics. 
CP-Gen~\cite{lin2025cpgen} integrates kinematic boundaries with contact dynamics for one-shot trajectory augmentation. 
Yang et al.~\cite{yang2025physicsdriven} combine VR human demonstrations, retargeting, and trajectory optimization to maintain dynamic feasibility across embodiments. 
CyberDemo~\cite{wang2024cyberdemo} transfers simulated human demonstrations to real-world dexterous policies via large-scale augmentation in simulation, using minimal real data.
Beyond geometric feasibility, RoCoDA uses symmetry to distinguish perturbations that leave the policy invariant from those that induce equivariant action changes.

\subsubsection{Correction and Recovery Augmentation}\label{sec:correction_recovery}

Most datasets lack failure-recovery examples, making policies fragile when they encounter Out-of-distribution (OOD) cases. 
DMD~\cite{zhang2024dmd} uses diffusion models to synthesize corrective observations from perturbed states, enabling recovery without physical collection. 
Complementing these offline methods, 
IntervenGen~\cite{pinto2024intervengen} and CR-DAgger~\cite{xu2025compliant} systematize online human intervention during autonomous rollouts, so that sparse human corrections can be used to train policies to recover from mistakes.

\subsubsection{Data Curation and Verification}\label{sec:data_curation}

As robot datasets scale in size and heterogeneity, the bottleneck shifts from data quantity to data utility.
Existing methods differ primarily in how they estimate data value. 
ReMix~\cite{hejna2025remix} learns how to weight different dataset domains during pretraining so as to improve robustness across downstream domains.
SCIZOR~\cite{zhang2025scizor} prunes transitions that are weakly informative for task progress or redundant with other samples.
CUPID~\cite{agia2025cupidcuratingdatarobot} uses influence estimates to assess the contribution of each demonstration to a policy's closed-loop performance, and then uses these estimates for filtering and data selection.

\subsection{Data Pipeline}\label{sec:data_factories_robotic_arms}

In current practice, \(\mathcal{D}\) is increasingly managed over a unified lifecycle, where collection, generation, augmentation, and refinement are treated as parts of one integrated production lifecycle.
Existing systems reflect different ways of organizing this full lifecycle of \(\mathcal{D}\). 
RoboTwin~\cite{mu2025robotwin} keeps synthetic data tethered to measured physical setups by matching dual-arm kinematics and sensor noise, and its second version adds task synthesis for automatic scenario generation. 
RoboVerse~\cite{geng2025roboverse} addresses fragmentation across simulators and datasets by providing a standardized MetaSim backbone spanning many tasks and assets. 
At larger scale, AgiBot World Colosseo~\cite{agibot2025} couples standardized large-scale real-robot collection with a generalist policy designed to better exploit heterogeneous data sources,
while NVIDIA Isaac GR00T~\cite{nvidia_gr00t} packages synthetic data generation, gathering, refinement, and sim-to-real alignment into a humanoid stack built on Isaac infrastructure. 
Supporting these pipelines, AIRSPEED~\cite{xia2024airspeed} decouples collection and generation from specific hardware backends, 
LeRobot~\cite{lerobot2024} turns fragmented real-world trajectories into reusable repositories, 
and MimicLabs~\cite{saxena2025matters} studies how quantity, diversity, and quality trade off as pipelines scale.
Taken together, these infrastructures move \(\mathcal{D}\) production toward automated and reproducible workflows.

\section{Learning Paradigms for Robotic Arm Manipulation}\label{sec:embodiment_method}

Learning paradigms are built on \(\mathcal{D}\), introduced in Section~\ref{sec:eai_datasets}, from which they acquire the knowledge support \(\mathcal{K}\) required for robotic manipulation under embodiment constraints. 
In this sense, learning is the stage at which embodied intelligence becomes operational in robotic arm manipulation. 
Robotic arm manipulation can be organized into two primary learning paradigms according to how \(\mathcal{K}\) is acquired and organized: cognitive planning, denoted by \(B\), and policy learning, denoted by \(P\).
This distinction is reflected in the components of $\mathcal{D}$ that each paradigm primarily emphasizes. 
Cognitive planning (\(B\)) relies primarily on sensory observations and state transitions to construct intermediate artifacts and specify actions, whereas policy learning (\(P\)) relies primarily on contact events and motion sequences to produce executable motor behavior under different action representations. 
Integration models couple these two pathways into unified architectures, either explicitly or implicitly.
As \(\mathcal{D}\) expands from local interaction traces to task-level execution and cross-embodiment experience, the knowledge support \(\mathcal{K}\) acquired from it also expands in scope.
We distinguish three corresponding levels of \(\mathcal{K}\):
$\mathcal{K}_1$ supports learning local interaction, $\mathcal{K}_2$ supports learning task structure for execution, and $\mathcal{K}_3$ supports learning transferable structure across embodiments. 
Section~\ref{sec:embodied_brain} introduces cognitive planning paradigms, Section~\ref{sec:policy_learning_low_level} covers policy learning paradigms, Section~\ref{sec:integration_models} examines how the two pathways are coupled, and Section~\ref{sec:deployment_pipeline} discusses deployment through a three-stage pipeline. 
Figure~\ref{fig:learning_paradigms} summarizes this learning ecosystem.

\begin{figure}[!t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\linewidth,trim=2cm 4.5cm 2.2cm 1cm,clip]{assets/learningparadigms.pdf}
  \caption{Overview of learning paradigms for robotic arm manipulation. The figure organizes the pipeline into cognitive planning, policy learning, integration models, and deployment stages, linked by multimodal inputs and downstream control across robotic arm embodiments.}
  \Description{A diagram showing the organization of learning paradigms for robotic manipulation. The top level shows cognitive planning methods ranging from semantic logic to generative dynamics. The middle level shows policy learning approaches covering explicit continuous, discrete action, and iterative generative methods. The bottom level shows integration architectures that couple planning and policy. Arrows indicate connections between data tiers, learning paradigms, and deployment stages.}\label{fig:learning_paradigms}
\end{figure}

\subsection{Cognitive Planning: The Embodied Brain}\label{sec:embodied_brain}

Cognitive planning places greater emphasis on sensory observations and state transitions in \(\mathcal{D}\), from which it acquires the support needed to construct intermediate artifacts.
The primary differences among these methods lie in the intermediate artifacts through which that support is organized. 
We therefore classify cognitive planning into five paradigms (B1--B5) according to the primary intermediate artifact each paradigm produces.
B1 produces semantic specifications that are directly usable for execution.
B2 organizes intent into logical task structures.
B3 grounds intent in embodied context through intermediate task representations.
B4 outputs spatial representations for direct execution.
B5 imagines future dynamics for action evaluation.
Among these paradigms, B1, B2, and B5 place greater emphasis on state transitions in \(\mathcal{D}\), because their intermediate artifacts are organized around task progression and future evolution, whereas B3 and B4 place greater emphasis on sensory observations, because their intermediate artifacts are grounded in the current embodied scene.
Meanwhile, the intermediate artifacts associated with B1--B5 collectively suggest a broader trend toward forms that are increasingly grounded and closer to execution.
Table~\ref{tab:brain_paradigms} summarizes these paradigms, while Figure~\ref{fig:brain_paradigms} illustrates the overall organization within the learning pipeline.

\begin{figure}[!t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.95\linewidth,trim=1cm 0cm 0cm 0.8cm,clip]{assets/brain.pdf}
  \caption{Cognitive planning paradigms for embodied manipulation. The figure summarizes five representative planning modes: semantic logic and code generation, long-horizon task decomposition, grounded multimodal reasoning, geometric grounding and spatial intelligence, and generative dynamics and physical imagination.}
  \Description{A diagram illustrating five cognitive planning paradigms for embodied manipulation. A central brain icon is surrounded by five labeled panels: semantic logic and code generation, long-horizon task decomposition, grounded multimodal reasoning, geometric grounding and spatial intelligence, and generative dynamics and physical imagination. The panels use a broccoli-into-pot task to show how each paradigm represents planning differently, from symbolic code and step decomposition to affordance maps, point clouds, and imagined future states.}\label{fig:brain_paradigms}
\end{figure}

\subsubsection{Semantic Logic and Code Generation}\label{sec:code_gen}

B1 is closest to the semantic interface of MLLMs, outputting executable specifications such as code, reward functions, or skill rankings. 
Early work framed this as probabilistic alignment between language and affordance. 
SayCan~\cite{brohan2023can} grounds abstract plans by scoring primitive skills against both semantic relevance and execution feasibility, but is limited to atomic actions without compositional logic. 
Program synthesis goes further. CaP~\cite{liang2022code} generates executable Python from natural language, and ProgPrompt~\cite{singh2022progprompt} embeds precondition assertions directly into the prompt structure to enforce logical constraints. 
A parallel line generates objective functions and leaves motor optimization to downstream solvers; Tan et al.~\cite{tan2023l2r} synthesizes programmatic reward functions that decouple semantic task specification from continuous trajectory optimization. 
Text2Touch~\cite{field2025text2touch} extends this paradigm to tactile dexterous manipulation, using LLM to design reward functions over tactile and proprioceptive signals for real-world manipulation. 
For structured control flow, LLM-BT~\cite{zhou2024llmbt} generates behavior trees that can be dynamically updated under environmental changes, enabling adaptive execution and recovery. 
LLM-as-BT~\cite{ao2025llmbt} generates modular behavior trees that support reactive switching and error recovery. 
Demo2Code~\cite{wang2023demo2code} extends the input modality by translating visual demonstrations into symbolic code, grounding program synthesis in physical observation alongside language. 
B1 primarily formulate task structure in the form of executable semantic specifications.
For this reason, they mainly require \(\mathcal{K}_2\) as their primary knowledge support. 

\subsubsection{Long-Horizon Task Decomposition}\label{sec:task_decomp}

B2 converts intent into logical task structures, decomposing long-horizon goals into ordered subtask sequences or formal plans. 
LLM+P~\cite{liu2023llmp} addresses this by translating natural language into PDDL and delegating reasoning to classical solvers that guarantee feasibility. 
L3M+P~\cite{agarwal2025l3mp} further enables the solver to learn domain transition models from experience, reducing reliance on pre-defined rules.  
Classical solvers, however, face combinatorial explosions in large environments. SayPlan~\cite{rana2023sayplan} mitigates this by anchoring search in 3D scene graphs, hierarchically pruning irrelevant regions before planning.
Inner Monologue~\cite{huang2023inner} extends this paradigm to replanning with feedback, while Statler~\cite{yoneda2024statler} maintains a task-relevant world state throughout execution.
Since they depend on the organization of long-horizon task structure and subtask relations, they primarily require \(\mathcal{K}_2\) as well.
B1 and B2 paradigms share the limitation that their representations are insufficiently grounded in real physical execution.

\subsubsection{Grounded Multimodal Representation}\label{sec:multimodal_reasoning}

B3 grounds high-level task intent into intermediate task formulations that can be mapped onto executable skill APIs.
PaLM-E~\cite{driess2023palme} injects visual observations and continuous robot representation directly into the LLM embedding space, so that semantic reasoning is conditioned on the current embodied scene.
EmbodiedGPT~\cite{mu2023embodiedgpt} extends this interface into the temporal domain by reasoning step by step with current observations in the loop, making task progression and intermediate subgoals more explicit.
ECoT~\cite{zawalski2025ecot} externalizes richer intermediate traces, including task descriptions, object grounding, and plans every step, before action prediction.
RoboBrain~\cite{ji2025robobrain} pushes this paradigm toward a more integrated manipulation-oriented interface by jointly modeling planning, affordance, trajectory, and longer-horizon visual context.
The primary knowledge support of B3 shifts from \(\mathcal{K}_2\), associated with task-conditioned scene understanding, toward \(\mathcal{K}_3\), associated with more transferable multimodal structure.

\subsubsection{Geometric Grounding and Spatial Intelligence}\label{sec:affordance_mapping}

B4 outputs spatial representations, including contact regions, 6-DoF poses, affordance maps, and neural fields, that planners and controllers can consume directly. 
ManipLLM~\cite{li2024manipllm} predicts pixel-wise contact masks from language, 
and A3VLM~\cite{huang2024a3vlm} extends this grounding to articulated objects by inferring joint axes and motion types. 
For 2D affordance mapping, CLIPort~\cite{shridhar2022cliport} combines semantic grounding with spatially precise manipulation for language-conditioned robot control., 
while PASG~\cite{zhu2025pasg} strengthens the mapping with functional keypoints. 
For 3D affordance mapping, F3RM~\cite{shen2023distilled} injects open-vocabulary semantics into neural feature fields, 
while D3Fields~\cite{wang2025d3fields} extends this line with dynamic 3D descriptor fields that encode semantic features and instance masks and support goal specification from 2D images. 
StructDiffusion~\cite{liu2022structdiffusion} similarly maps high-level language goals and partial scene observations to physically valid target object arrangements for downstream execution.
VoxPoser~\cite{huang2023voxposer} and ReKep~\cite{huang2024rekep} push this line further by turning language or task descriptions into explicit 3D value maps and spatio-temporal relational keypoint constraints for downstream optimization.
Across these methods, the common objective is to expose enough spatial structure for manipulation to query and optimize at execution time. 
B4 methods primarily represent the current geometric state of interaction, and therefore mainly require \(\mathcal{K}_1\). 
When extended toward high-level constraint reasoning, they increasingly require \(\mathcal{K}_2\).
Therefore, B4 representations cannot adequately capture state changes during contact, dynamic interaction, or long-horizon consequences.

\subsubsection{Generative Dynamics and Physical Imagination}\label{sec:generative_dynamics}

B5 comprises predictive planning artifacts, in which candidate actions are evaluated through explicit forecasts or imagined rollouts of future states, observations, or latent world dynamics. 
One route uses learned predictive models as the substrate for imagination. 
UniPi~\cite{du2023unipi} reformulates policy as future video generation, UWM~\cite{zhu2025uwm} unifies video and action diffusion within one transformer, and V-JEPA2~\cite{assran2025vjepa} shifts from pixel reconstruction to latent predictive reasoning. 
Purely learned prediction, however, drifts from physical consistency over long horizons. 
A second line constrains prediction with structural priors. Schulze et al.~\cite{schulze2025lagrangian} inject analytical mechanics into learned dynamics, Zhang et al.~\cite{zhang2025particlegrid} targets deformable interaction through particle-mesh structure, and LUMOS~\cite{nematollahi2025lumos} uses language instructions to guide predictive rollouts in the learned world model.
B5 methods primarily require \(\mathcal{K}_2\) when they are used to organize state transitions through predictive rollout, they sometimes extend toward \(\mathcal{K}_3\) by modeling broader latent predictive structure. 
However, B5 is limited by the reliability of predictive modeling itself, errors accumulate in latent rollout, making it difficult to maintain stability over long horizons.

\subsection{Policy Learning: The Embodied Cerebellum}\label{sec:policy_learning_low_level}

Policy learning maps perceptual observations to motor commands, thus focusing more on the contact events and motion sequences in \(\mathcal{D}\). 
We organize these methods into three categories by how the action space is represented. 
P1 policies output continuous action vectors via regression in a single forward pass.
P2 policies quantize actions into tokens and generate them autoregressively via cross-entropy. 
P3 policies model the full action distribution through multi-step denoising or flow matching. 
Within these three categories, sub-directions further distinguish how policy generation is factorized in practice, how it handles distribution shift, and how it generalizes across embodiments.
Table~\ref{tab:policy_paradigms} summarizes overview of this kind of paradigms,and Figure~\ref{fig:policy_paradigms} contrasts these paradigms.

\subsubsection{Explicit Continuous Policies}\label{sec:explicit_continuous_policies}

\begin{figure}[!t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth,trim=3cm 0.5cm 3.5cm 0.3cm,clip]{assets/celeberrum.pdf}
  \caption{Comparison of policy learning paradigms for robotic manipulation. The figure contrasts explicit continuous policies, discrete action policies, and iterative generative policies in terms of distributional expressivity and computational complexity.}\label{fig:policy_paradigms}
  \Description{A comparison diagram of three policy learning paradigms for robotic manipulation. The figure is arranged along two axes: computational complexity increases from left to right, and distribution expressivity increases from top to bottom. The top row compares explicit continuous policies and discrete-action policies, while the bottom row illustrates iterative generative policies based on diffusion and flow matching.}
\end{figure}

P1 policies map observations directly to continuous action vectors.
The basic design separates a pre-trained visual encoder from a lightweight action head. 
BC-Z~\cite{jang2022bc} uses this pattern for multi-task zero-shot generalization, MVP~\cite{xiao2022mvp} shows that masked visual pre-training yields representations robust enough to reduce the policy to a simple MLP, and PointPolicy~\cite{haldar2025pointpolicy} extends explicit regression to 3D point clouds. 
Single-step prediction, however, ignores trajectory-level context. Decision Transformer~\cite{chen2021decisiontransformer} instead reframes control as autoregressive sequence modeling with a regression objective, while ACT-style methods~\cite{zhao2023learning, fu2024mobile} combine action chunking with CVAE-based modeling to predict short-horizon action sequences and reduce jitter in bimanual and mobile manipulation.
BAKU~\cite{haldar2024baku} combines CNN-based feature extraction with a transformer policy for multi-task settings. 
To handle distribution shift, MimicPlay~\cite{wang2023mimicplay} maps human play data to guide low-level visuomotor control trained on a small number of teleoperated demonstrations, Iterative Residual Policy~\cite{chi2022iterativeresidualpolicy} predicts residual corrections to recover from execution drift, and Motion Tracks~\cite{ren2025motion_tracks} learns a unified motion representation for cross-embodiment adaptation. 
Within P1, reactive local control is grounded in local interaction and therefore primarily depends on \(\mathcal{K}_1\). Task-conditioned, chunked policies, by contrast, rely more on task-level execution structure and accordingly require \(\mathcal{K}_2\) more strongly.
Despite their simplicity and efficiency, explicit continuous policies are poorly matched to multimodal action distributions, as a single deterministic prediction cannot capture multiple valid behaviors.

\subsubsection{Discrete Action Policies}\label{sec:discrete_action_policies}

P2 policies discretize continuous actions into tokens and model them autoregressively, enabling multimodal action generation beyond unimodal regression.
Early approaches employ uniform discretization. 
RT-1~\cite{brohan2023rt1} maps continuous dimensions to 256 bins, and PerAct~\cite{shridhar2022peract} extends this to 3D by classifying actions over voxelized grids.
However, fixed discretization imposes a rigid token space that is often poorly matched to continuous action distributions, motivating learned discretization methods that adapt the token space to the data.
Learned discretization addresses this limitation. BeT~\cite{shafiullah2022bet} derives action modes through k-means clustering, VQ-BeT~\cite{lee2024vqbet} learns a compact discrete action vocabulary with VQ-VAE, and CARP~\cite{gong2025carp} further improves action prediction through multi-scale autoregressive modeling.
Once actions are reliably tokenized, they can be generated within the same autoregressive framework used for text. VIMA~\cite{jiang2023vima} interleaves visual objects with text instructions for multimodal prompt conditioning, GR-1~\cite{wu2023gr1} unifies vision, language, and action in a single stream, Seer~\cite{tian2024seer} attributes much of the gain to the discrete formulation itself, and ARP~\cite{zhang2025arp} shows that chunk-level autoregressive generation generalizes across diverse robots. ICIL~\cite{fu2025icil} further treats imitation as sequence completion, adapting to unseen tasks by conditioning on demonstration prompts without gradient updates.
Within P2, task-scale discrete action generation is organized around task-level action structure and therefore primarily requires \(\mathcal{K}_2\). 
Multimodal and generalist token policies increasingly require \(\mathcal{K}_3\) when they need to organize transferable action structure across heterogeneous embodiments.
The central challenge in these methods lies in aligning discrete action tokens with the structured, continuous nature of robot actions, while preserving action fidelity and efficient autoregressive generation.

\subsubsection{Iterative Generative Policies}\label{sec:iterative_generative_policies}

P3 policies treat action prediction as conditional generative modeling over continuous action trajectories, using iterative refinement to approximate the full action distribution.
Early diffusion-based formulations first appeared in planning and structured generation: Diffuser~\cite{janner2022diffuser} casts long-horizon trajectory generation as probabilistic inpainting. 
Diffusion Policy~\cite{chi2023diffusion} subsequently brings this paradigm into mainstream visuomotor control by learning the score function of the action distribution, thereby avoiding the mode-averaging limitations of direct regression. 
In 3D robotic arm manipulation, DP3~\cite{ze2024diffusion} further conditions diffusion on point-cloud observations for spatial grounding, while iDP3~\cite{liang2024idp3} extends this formulation with improved 3D representations for more robust deployment.
Despite these advances, iterative denoising still imposes substantial inference latency.
Flow matching addresses this by learning velocity fields that transport noise to data along straighter trajectories with fewer integration steps. 
Consistency Policy~\cite{prasad2024consistency} distills a teacher diffusion model into few-step generation, Streaming Flow Policy~\cite{jiang2025streaming} streams action trajectories to the robot during generation, and RecFlow~\cite{xue2025recflow} accelerates inference by rectifying the learned flow itself.
Among generalist robot policies, Octo~\cite{octomodelteam2024} trains a transformer-based diffusion policy on the Open X-Embodiment dataset for cross-embodiment manipulation, RDT-1B~\cite{liu2025rdtb} scales to the 1B-parameter regime on larger multi-robot data, and FLOWER~\cite{reuss2025flower} improves sample efficiency through flow-matching.
Within P3, local generative control is grounded in continuous interaction and therefore primarily requires \(\mathcal{K}_1\). Larger-scale generalist generative policies, however, require \(\mathcal{K}_3\) to the extent that they are used to capture transferable action regularities across embodiments, rather than merely benefiting from large-scale pretraining.
Overall, P3 policies offer the strongest distributional modeling capacity, but their practicality depends on making iterative generative control efficient enough for deployment while preserving action quality, control stability, and manageable compute cost.

\subsection{Integration Models}\label{sec:integration_models}

To learn from the full content of \(\mathcal{D}\), robotic arm manipulation systems often integrate cognitive planning and policy learning rather than relying on either path alone.
RT-2~\cite{brohan2023rt2} provided an early demonstration that semantic understanding, reasoning, and action generation can be unified in this way, and later work has evolved into five recurrent integration patterns, which can be grouped into three coupling types.
Type~I emphasizes end-to-end unification within a shared backbone, as discussed in Section~\ref{subsec:e2emodels}.
Type~II covers hierarchical designs that separate slower reasoning from faster execution, as discussed in Section~\ref{subsec:hierarchical_modular_models}.
Type~III maintains a shared world representation that mediates between planning and policy, as discussed in Section~\ref{subsec:spatial_world_models}.
Table~\ref{tab:data_learning_map} summarizes these recurrent patterns in terms of architectural type, supporting data regime, coupling principle, shared state, and typical pairings of cognitive planning and policy learning paradigms, and Table~\ref{tab:integration_methods} further lists representative integrated paradigms.

\subsubsection{Type I\@: Unified End-to-End Foundation Models}\label{subsec:e2emodels}

Type I models treat actions as another modality tokenized alongside text and image patches, absorbing semantic understanding, reasoning, and action generation into a single backbone. 
Early paradigms represented actions as discrete tokens. RT-2~\cite{brohan2023rt2} co-fine-tuned a VLM to output discrete action tokens, and OpenVLA~\cite{kim2024openvla} released a 7B model trained on 970k Open X-Embodiment trajectories. X-VLA~\cite{zheng2026xvla} continues this line with embodiment-specific soft prompts inside a shared transformer, showing how a unified backbone can extend across multiple robot platforms without introducing an explicit hierarchical split. Continuous representations followed. $\pi_0$~\cite{black2024pi0} introduced flow matching for high-frequency dexterous control, and RDT-2~\cite{liu2026rdt2} extends this to bimanual manipulation with zero-shot cross-embodiment deployment. RoboMamba~\cite{liu2024robomamba} replaces self-attention with State Space Models for linear-time processing, and auxiliary reconstructive objectives encode 3D spatial structure to compensate for weak explicit geometry~\cite{song2025reconvla}.
Although Type I paradigms demonstrate strong benefits from unifying perception, semantic reasoning, and action generation within a single architecture, this unification also comes at a cost.
By forcing cognitive planning and motor control into the same representational and computational loop, Type I couples processes that operate at very different temporal and physical scales. 
The central challenge of this type is whether a single unified architecture can effectively support both high-level reasoning and real-time control.

\subsubsection{Type II\@: Hierarchical \& Modular Reasoning Models}\label{subsec:hierarchical_modular_models}

Type II models decouple slow cognitive planning from fast policy execution. One branch uses explicit hierarchy. 
RT-H~\cite{belkhale2024rth} introduced language motions as an intermediate layer between high-level instructions and low-level actions, 
CoT-VLA~\cite{zhao2025cotvla} and ThinkAct~\cite{huang2025thinkact} expose intermediate reasoning steps, 
RoBridge~\cite{zhang2025robridge} places a symbolic bridge between a cognitive planner and a learned policy, 
and HAMSTER~\cite{li2025hamster} predicts a coarse 2D path as an intermediate representation to guide a low-level policy for precise 3D manipulation.
Another branch uses implicit hierarchy, where the practical split is clear but the interface is latent or only lightly structured. 
FiS-VLA~\cite{chen2025fis} couples a slower semantic branch with a real-time execution branch, 
Hi Robot~\cite{shi2025hirobot} adopts a hierarchical paradigm design for open-ended instruction following and user feedback during execution, 
and GO-1~\cite{agibot2025} scales the same design to industrial bimanual control.
Although explicit and implicit Type II models offer advantages, their drawbacks are also evident.
Explicit hierarchies risk forcing task-relevant information into fixed intermediate representations, which can lead to the loss of execution-sensitive information.
Implicit hierarchies, by contrast, can hide coordination across levels in opaque internal states, making them difficult to supervise or correct.
Their common bottleneck lies in the decomposition itself: once slow planning is separated from fast control, the interface between planning and policy becomes the critical constraint on information flow, error correction, and cross-level coordination.

\subsubsection{Type III\@: Spatial Grounding \& Predictive World Models}\label{subsec:spatial_world_models}

Type III maintains a shared world representation that planning and policy jointly consume. 
This world state takes two forms. 
Spatial grounding paradigms build a persistent geometric representation: 3D-VLA~\cite{zhen20243dvla} embeds 3D perception into the LLM for spatial reasoning, SpatialVLA~\cite{qu2025spatialvla} grounds actions in metric 3D structure via Ego3D Position Encoding, TraceVLA~\cite{zheng2025tracevla} conditions generation on visual trajectory traces, and BridgeVLA~\cite{li2025bridgevla} aligns spatial and predictive representations to ensure kinematic feasibility. 
Predictive world models instead learn forward dynamics from data: VideoVLA~\cite{shen2025videovla} jointly predicts action sequences and future visual outcomes using a pre-trained video generator, DreamVLA~\cite{zhang2025dreamvla} integrates a diffusion-based world model into the manipulation pipeline for planning via imagined trajectories, and DreamZero~\cite{ye2026dreamzero} learns a joint predictive model over future visual states and actions.
Although Type III models exhibit strong potential, they rely heavily on a reliable shared world representation. 
If this representation is inaccurate or stale, the entire system can be misled. 
While correction is possible, it often incurs substantial computational and engineering cost. 
In addition, Type III models typically demand large-scale data and resources to train, and still face major deployment bottlenecks in inference cost, model drift, and reliable closed-loop execution.

\midrule

 & RL Fine-Tuning & Direct GRPO, world-model-guided adaptation & SimpleVLA-RL~\cite{li2026simplevlarl}, DiWA~\cite{chandra2025diwa} \\
 & Contact-Rich Adapt. & Consistency policies, visuo-tactile refinement & ConRFT~\cite{chen2025conrft}, VT-Refine~\cite{huang2025vtrefine} \\

 & Quantization & Action-centric mixed-precision, 1-bit PTQ & QVLA~\cite{xu2026qvla} \\

 & Robustness \& Safety & Contrastive decoding, motion planning & Policy CD~\cite{wu2026policycontrastive}, Neural MP~\cite{dalal2024neuralmp} \\

\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

The embodiment foundations define the feasible physical space that constrains how manipulation capability can be realized. 
In practice, many methods introduce additional processing during deployment before learned models can be executed reliably in the real world. 
These processing steps are increasingly being organized into a deployment pipeline.
Before deployment, models are refined and prepared for physical rollout through offline adaptation, transfer, and compression.
During deployment, they are further aligned to the target hardware and interaction conditions.
At runtime, they are executed under real-world constraints with support for efficient, robust, and safe control.
Table~\ref{tab:deployment_lifecycle} summarizes the main bottlenecks and representative techniques at each stage.

\subsubsection{Stage 1: Offline Pre-deployment}\label{subsec:pre_deployment}

Before physical deployment, the main challenges are transferring policies from simulation to reality, generalizing across embodiments, and making large models practical for real-world execution.
Q-Transformer~\cite{chebotar2023qtransformer} shows that autoregressive Q-functions can scale offline actor-critic learning to transformer policies through temporal difference backups on static datasets.
X-Sim~\cite{dan2025xsim} uses a real-to-sim-to-real pipeline to learn cross-embodiment manipulation from human videos by treating object motion as a dense and transferable signal.
ASAP~\cite{he2025asap} mitigates dynamics mismatch between simulation and reality by learning a residual action compensation model that aligns simulated dynamics with real-world execution.
TRANSIC~\cite{jiang2025transic} adopts a complementary human-in-the-loop strategy in which operators provide online corrections during real-world execution, from which a residual policy is learned and combined with the original simulation policy for autonomous deployment.
As model size grows, pre-deployment compression also becomes necessary: FAST~\cite{pertsch2025fast} improves deployment efficiency by replacing naive action discretization with a compressed action representation based on the discrete cosine transform.

\subsubsection{Stage 2: Online Physical Alignment}\label{subsec:online_alignment}

Once deployed on physical hardware, the primary bottleneck shifts from transfer preparation to adaptation under real contact, friction, sensing noise, and OOD objects. 
SimpleVLA-RL~\cite{li2026simplevlarl} shows that large manipulation models can be fine-tuned directly through reinforcement learning, 
FLaRe~\cite{hu2024flare} addresses the tension between generalist priors and specialist performance through adaptive large-scale RL fine-tuning, 
while DiWA~\cite{chandra2025diwa} adapts diffusion policies through an offline world model, reducing the amount of physical interaction needed for post-training improvement. 
ConRFT~\cite{chen2025conrft} combines offline BC and Q-learning with online consistency policies and human intervention checkpoints to support safe adaptation in contact-rich settings. 
Human-in-the-loop real-world reinforcement learning provides another effective route for this stage: 
HIL-SERL~\cite{luo2025humaninloop} combines demonstrations, learned reward modeling, and online human corrections to learn precise and dexterous manipulation skills directly on physical robots.
VT-Refine~\cite{huang2025vtrefine} further demonstrates that visuo-tactile policies, when paired with tactile digital twins and refinement in simulation, can improve high-precision dual-arm coordination. Other alignment strategies include residual adaptation via off-policy RL~\cite{xiao2025pld}. 
The adaptation choices made here directly affect Stage~3: aggressive full-parameter fine-tuning can improve task performance but complicate compression and serving, whereas residual or modular updates preserve more of the original policy structure at the cost of additional execution-time overhead.

\subsubsection{Stage 3: Inference-Time Execution}\label{subsec:inference_execution}

During real-world deployment, the key challenge is to sustain reliable closed-loop execution on edge hardware.
QVLA~\cite{xu2026qvla} addresses action-sensitive quantization through channel-wise bit allocation, assigning higher precision to channels that are more sensitive in the action space. 
TinyVLA~\cite{wen2025tinyvla} shows that compact paradigm design can improve both inference speed and data efficiency, even eliminating the need for a separate large-scale pretraining stage, while PD-VLA~\cite{pdvla2025} accelerates chunked action generation by replacing sequential autoregressive decoding with parallel decoding iterations.
Runtime robustness is equally critical. 
Policy Contrastive Decoding~\cite{wu2026policycontrastive} mitigates sensitivity to irrelevant visual features during action generation, 
FAIL-Detect~\cite{xu2025detectfailures} reframes runtime failure detection as sequential OOD detection without requiring failure data, and Neural MP~\cite{dalal2024neuralmp} improves execution reliability by distilling classical motion planning into a fast reactive neural planner that generalizes across cluttered real-world settings. Reflective TTP~\cite{hong2026rttp} supports online recovery through reflection and updates during test, re-evaluating actions with hindsight to adapt the policy during deployment.

Despite progress across all three stages, current methods still face several limitations. 
Pre-deployment approaches are often sensitive to simulator fidelity, offline data coverage, and the scope of embodiment variation, which limits the robustness of transfer to open-ended real-world settings. 
Online physical alignment methods improve adaptation under real dynamics, but they often require expensive robot interaction, careful reward or intervention design, and, in many cases, substantial human involvement that may hinder scalability. 
At inference time, compression, quantization, and decoding acceleration improve deployability, yet their impact on fine-grained action fidelity and contact-rich control are insufficiently understood.

\section{System Evaluation for Robotic Arm Manipulation}\label{sec:evaluation}

Previous sections have examined how robotic arm manipulation systems are built by embodied foundations, data ecosystems, and learning paradigms. 
This chapter reviews current efforts to evaluate robotic arm manipulation systems from four corresponding perspectives.
Section~\ref{sec:eval_task_execution} evaluates whether cognitive planning (\(B\)) and policy learning (\(P\)) can organize the knowledge support \(\mathcal{K}\) induced by \(\mathcal{D}\) into successful task execution. 
Section~\ref{sec:eval_data_sample} evaluates whether \(\mathcal{D}\) provides data that is sufficiently efficient and informative to induce useful knowledge support \(\mathcal{K}\). 
Section~\ref{sec:eval_benchmarking} evaluates whether the knowledge support \(\mathcal{K}\) learned from \(\mathcal{D}\), and organized through \(B\) and \(P\), is robust beyond the training distribution under varying embodiment constraints. 
Section~\ref{sec:eval_energy} evaluates whether the physically grounded capabilities produced by \(B\) and \(P\) from \(\mathcal{K}\) can be executed efficiently on physical embodiments within the deployment pipeline.
Table~\ref{tab:eval_metrics} summarizes representative metrics and protocols.
